info.title: codeitall — Rust agent deployment surface
info.description: Phase 1.11 deployment surface. Five tools served deterministically from a Postgres-backed Rust substrate (66.5M pattern_records, 7 probe families). No generative LLM in the request-response path; bge-m3 cosine fallback is deterministic-inference-only per vision non-negotiable #9.

post.description: Zero-friction key issuance per locked OQ-1.11.C=C1+C4. No email, no captcha, no signup. Per-IP issuance cap of 10/day; per-key baseline quota 10K queries/day. The raw key is returned exactly once.

Use when the agent already knows the signature shape it wants — e.g. `fn(&str) -> Result<Vec<u8>, _>`, `fn(&[u8]) -> Result<String, std::str::Utf8Error>`, or `impl Iterator<Item=Result<...>>`. Returns ranked candidate functions with the crate they live in, downloads, yank status, and a compact behavior summary if probed. Pass the exact signature verbatim; the substrate normalises whitespace + identifiers on its end.

shape.description: Signature shape verbatim (exact-match), e.g. 'fn(&str) -> bool'. No wildcards.

shape_b64.description: base64url of the signature — agent-safe (no URL special characters to re-encode). Prefer this from GET-only / browse clients.

get.description:
Use when the agent already knows the signature shape it wants — e.g. `fn(&str) -> Result<Vec<u8>, _>`, `fn(&[u8]) -> Result<String, std::str::Utf8Error>`, or `impl Iterator<Item=Result<...>>`. Returns ranked candidate functions with the crate they live in, downloads, yank status, and a compact behavior summary if probed. Pass the exact signature verbatim; the substrate normalises whitespace + identifiers on its end.

GET alias for browse-only callers: pass the same fields as query parameters.

schema.description: Signature shape verbatim (exact-match), e.g. 'fn(&str) -> bool'. No wildcards.
base64url of the signature — agent-safe (no URL special characters to re-encode). Prefer this from GET-only / browse clients.

post.description: Use when the agent has a specific (crate, fn_name) pair and wants to know what inputs it actually accepts at runtime — e.g. `(crate='jiff', fn_name='Timestamp::from_str')`. Returns the probe observation table verbatim from the substrate: each row is (input, outcome=ok|err|panic, value or error variant). Pass an optional `inputs` array to filter to specific input strings. The substrate's discrimination findings live here — runtime behaviour the docs are silent or wrong about.

get.description:
Use when the agent has a specific (crate, fn_name) pair and wants to know what inputs it actually accepts at runtime — e.g. `(crate='jiff', fn_name='Timestamp::from_str')`. Returns the probe observation table verbatim from the substrate: each row is (input, outcome=ok|err|panic, value or error variant). Pass an optional `inputs` array to filter to specific input strings. The substrate's discrimination findings live here — runtime behaviour the docs are silent or wrong about.

GET alias for browse-only callers: pass the same fields as query parameters.

post.description: Use when the agent asks about a task category — e.g. 'how do I parse JSON in rust', 'compare base64 crates', 'which datetime library handles RFC 3339 timezones right' — and wants the cross-implementation behavior table. The substrate returns side-by-side observations on the canonical input set: for each implementation (crate, fn_name), each input in the family's input set is paired with the observed (outcome, value_or_error_variant). Discrimination signal lives here — the docs-silent runtime behaviour pattern Guiding Principle #8 names.

task.description: Probe family name from the §27 catalogue (datetime, json_strict_vs_permissive, base64, …).

schema.description: Probe family name from the §27 catalogue (datetime, json_strict_vs_permissive, base64, …).

post.description: Use when the agent suspects the code it's about to write uses a deprecated API — e.g. `tokio::Runtime::new()` (now `tokio::runtime::Runtime::new()`), `chrono::DateTime::from_str` (now feature-gated), or any path that points to a yanked or RustSec-advisory'd crate version. The substrate returns the current canonical alternative with a behavior summary if probed, and surfaces yank-status / advisory rows verbatim.

get.description:
Use when the agent suspects the code it's about to write uses a deprecated API — e.g. `tokio::Runtime::new()` (now `tokio::runtime::Runtime::new()`), `chrono::DateTime::from_str` (now feature-gated), or any path that points to a yanked or RustSec-advisory'd crate version. The substrate returns the current canonical alternative with a behavior summary if probed, and surfaces yank-status / advisory rows verbatim.

GET alias for browse-only callers: pass the same fields as query parameters.

post.description: Use this when an agent asks open-ended Rust API questions like 'how do I parse a duration string', 'which crate gives me HMAC verification', 'is this function deprecated', 'what's the current way to do JWT auth', or 'compare base64 crates'. Hand off free-text questions verbatim; the substrate routes them deterministically via rule-based intent detection (no LLM in the request path) and dispatches into signature_search, behavior_lookup, compare_implementations, or find_modern_equivalent as appropriate, falling back to bge-m3 cosine retrieval if no rule matches. Returns a structured verdict with routed_via, the primary recommendation, evidence, and caveats.
